content.problemStatement:
Chef has 333 numbers PPP, QQQ and RRR. Chef wants to find the number of triples (A,B,C)(A, B, C)(A,B,C) such that:


(A∣B)=P,(A \mid B) = P,(A∣B)=P, (B∣C)=Q(B \mid C) = Q(B∣C)=Q and (C∣A)=R(C \mid A) = R(C∣A)=R (Here, ∣\mid∣ denotes the bitwise OR operation)
0≤A,B,C<2200 \le A, B, C \lt 2^{20}0≤A,B,C<220
Can you help Chef?


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of a single line of input containing 333 space-separated integers denoting P,QP, QP,Q and RRR respectively.


Output Format
For each test case, output a single integer denoting the number of triplets (A,B,C)(A, B, C)(A,B,C) that satisfy the given conditions.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains three space‑separated integers P, Q and R (0 ≤ P, Q, R < 2^20).
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the number of triples (A, B, C) with 0 ≤ A, B, C < 2^20 that satisfy A|B = P, B|C = Q and C|A = R.

sampleTestCases.explanation: For the first test case the per‑bit counts are 1,1,1,4 giving 4 total triples. The second case yields only one possible triple (0,0,5). The third case has a bit pattern (0,0,1) which is impossible, so the answer is 0.

solution.approach:
The three OR conditions are independent for each bit position because the OR operation works per bit. For a fixed bit i let p, q, r be the i‑th bits of P, Q, R. We need to count the number of assignments (a, b, c) ∈ {0,1}^3 such that a|b = p, b|c = q and c|a = r. By enumerating the 8 possible (a,b,c) we obtain:
- (p,q,r) = (0,0,0) → 1 way
- (p,q,r) = (0,1,1) → 1 way
- (p,q,r) = (1,1,0) → 1 way
- (p,q,r) = (1,0,1) → 1 way
- (p,q,r) = (1,1,1) → 4 ways
All other (p,q,r) triples are impossible (0 ways). The total number of triples for the whole numbers is the product of the per‑bit counts because bits are independent. If any bit yields 0 ways the answer is 0. The product never exceeds 4^20 ≈ 1.1·10^12, fitting in 64‑bit signed integer.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T * 20) ≈ O(T), because we examine 20 bits per test case.
